source_note: Curated supplement knowledge for on-device label review and Library display. Dose ranges describe published trial contexts, official reference thresholds, or commercial label discovery; they are not personal recommendations.

dose_contexts.population: adults 19-50, female-to-male range where sex differs
dose_contexts.interpretation: Compare the effective daily Vitamin A amount against the selected demographic in NRV data; this range is a quick adult context only.

dose_contexts.context: adult AU upper limit label-review threshold
dose_contexts.interpretation: Treat labels near or above this threshold as review flags; apply demographic-specific ULs in CalculationService.

clinical_notes.text: Use the observed label amount for Vitamin A; if OCR misses the amount, keep it unknown rather than converting it to zero.
Separate preformed retinol/retinyl esters from provitamin A carotenoids; the AU upper limit applies to preformed vitamin A, not beta-carotene.
High preformed vitamin A needs pregnancy and retinoid-medication review because excess retinol is clinically relevant.

dose_contexts.interpretation: No AU UL is established in the bundled NRV table; absence of a UL is not proof that high supplemental intake is risk-free.

The folate upper limit applies to synthetic folic acid from supplements or fortified foods, not naturally occurring food folate.
High folic acid can mask vitamin B12 deficiency; keep B12 status visible when high folic acid appears on a label.

clinical_notes.text: Use the observed label amount for Vitamin E; if OCR misses the amount, keep it unknown rather than converting it to zero.
Vitamin E IU conversion differs by natural d-alpha-tocopherol and synthetic dl-alpha-tocopherol; do not infer the form when the label is ambiguous.

Iron labels require child-safety and overdose awareness; high iron should not be treated as a benign wellness dose.

The adult AU upper limit applies to supplemental or medication magnesium, not food magnesium.
Prefer elemental magnesium amount when a label lists both compound weight and provided elemental amount.

Potassium supplements need medication and kidney-function review because ACE inhibitors, ARBs, potassium-sparing diuretics, and kidney disease can change risk.

dose_contexts.context: adult AU upper limit label-review threshold
dose_contexts.interpretation: Treat labels near or above this threshold as review flags; apply demographic-specific ULs in CalculationService.

clinical_notes.text: Use the observed label amount for Zinc; if OCR misses the amount, keep it unknown rather than converting it to zero.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Published black cohosh studies commonly use quantified root or rhizome extracts in this range; triterpene glycoside standardisation makes labels easier to compare.

clinical_notes.text: Liver symptoms, pregnancy, breastfeeding, and hormone-sensitive history need clinical review before interpreting a high-strength label.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Aged garlic extract, oil, and powder are different preparations; compare labels within the same preparation type.

clinical_notes.text: Anticoagulant, antiplatelet, HIV medication, and surgery contexts deserve medication review when a garlic label uses concentrated extract.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Published dose reviews and Australian labels commonly anchor interpretation to andrographolide content rather than dry-herb equivalent.

clinical_notes.text: Pregnancy, fertility treatment, autoimmune disease, allergy history, anticoagulants, immunosuppressants, and short-term high-strength use need review.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Cognition studies usually run for weeks and depend on bacoside standardisation; one-off doses should not be read the same way.

clinical_notes.text: Gastrointestinal effects, sedative overlap, thyroid medicines, pregnancy, and breastfeeding need review with concentrated bacopa extracts.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Osteoarthritis studies use different extracts, so AKBA or boswellic-acid standardisation gives the dose its meaning.

clinical_notes.text: Review pregnancy, breastfeeding, anticoagulant use, active gastrointestinal disease, and surgery plans with high-strength boswellia labels.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Concentrated extracts approaching this EGCG exposure need closer liver-safety review; brewed tea and extract labels are not interchangeable.

clinical_notes.text: Liver-safety review matters most for concentrated extracts, fasting use, multi-ingredient weight-loss products, and medicines with liver-risk overlap.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Mood and menopause studies often use low-milligram standardised extracts, so dry-herb equivalents can overstate comparability.

clinical_notes.text: Pregnancy, bipolar disorder, serotonergic medicines, anticoagulants, and high-dose saffron products need review.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Standard curcuminoid labels usually need higher milligram amounts than enhanced-absorption complexes; compare by curcuminoids, not only total turmeric equivalent.
Enhanced-absorption products such as Meriva may show lower curcumin mass with high dry-herb equivalents, so the form needs to stay attached to the dose.

clinical_notes.text: High-dose or concentrated curcumin deserves medication review with anticoagulants, antiplatelets, gallbladder disease, liver symptoms, or surgery plans.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Echinacea labels vary by species and plant part; root, aerial parts, and fresh-plant extracts belong in separate comparisons.

clinical_notes.text: Allergy history, autoimmune disease, immunosuppressant therapy, pregnancy, and multi-herb immune formulas deserve review.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Horsetail labels are most useful when they state silicon or silica standardisation; total extract mass alone has limited clinical comparability.

clinical_notes.text: Renal disease, pregnancy, thiamine status, diuretic use, and high-silica formulas deserve review before treating the dose as routine.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Most clinically interpretable labels specify 24% flavone glycosides and 6% terpene lactones; generic leaf powder has a different evidence context.

clinical_notes.text: Medication review is important with anticoagulants, antiplatelets, antiretrovirals, seizure history, and planned surgery.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Labels around this range are common in mood studies, but the interaction profile matters more than the dose alone.

clinical_notes.text: This herb can lower exposure to many medicines, including antidepressants, oral contraceptives, transplant medicines, anticoagulants, and HIV medicines.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Blood-pressure trials often use extract mass and oleuropein content; labels with only dry-leaf equivalent have weaker comparability.

clinical_notes.text: Medication review matters with antihypertensives, diabetes medicines, pregnancy, breastfeeding, and symptomatic low blood pressure.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Panax ginseng, American ginseng, and Siberian ginseng are different ingredients; labels should keep the species visible.

clinical_notes.text: Medication review matters with anticoagulants, antihypertensives, diabetes medicines, stimulants, statins, and antidepressants.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Labels with rosavin and salidroside percentages are easier to compare than root-equivalent claims alone.

clinical_notes.text: Review stimulant sensitivity, bipolar disorder, pregnancy, breastfeeding, and medicines affecting mood or sleep.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Most interpretable labels use a lipophilic berry extract standardised to fatty acids; whole berry powder is a different comparison.

clinical_notes.text: Prostate symptoms, PSA monitoring, anticoagulant or antiplatelet use, and surgery plans need clinical context.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Clinical comparison works best when the label states silymarin or silybin content, not only milk thistle dry-herb equivalent.

clinical_notes.text: Liver-health claims need clinical context; review allergy history, diabetes medicines, pregnancy, and active liver symptoms.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: PAC amount is more useful than cranberry fruit equivalent for urinary-tract labels; study results remain mixed by population and product.

clinical_notes.text: Warfarin interaction evidence is mixed, so high-PAC cranberry labels deserve medication review rather than an automatic contraindication.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Sleep labels should state root extract amount and timing; alcohol-containing tinctures are a separate preparation.

clinical_notes.text: Sedatives, alcohol, driving, pregnancy, breastfeeding, and surgery plans need review when a label contains valerian.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Trial interpretation depends on the named extract and fruit-extract amount; berry powder milligrams are not equivalent to Ze 440-style extracts.

clinical_notes.text: Pregnancy, breastfeeding, fertility treatment, dopamine-active medicines, hormone therapy, oral contraceptives, and pituitary disorders need review.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: This range fits common adult stress and sleep studies when the label states root extract and withanolide standardisation.

clinical_notes.text: Pregnancy, thyroid disease, autoimmune disease, sedative use, liver symptoms, and surgery plans need review before interpreting a high-withanolide label.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Ginger labels should separate extract mass from dry-rhizome equivalent and gingerol standardisation when shown.

clinical_notes.text: Anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy, gallbladder disease, pregnancy-specific dosing questions, and surgery plans need review.
Product claims become clinically comparable only when the scanned label provides dose, extract type, and standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Spore-forming probiotics need strain-level interpretation; CFU comparisons across Bacillus, Lactobacillus, and yeast products are not equivalent.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.interpretation: B. lactis products should be interpreted by strain code; HN019 and BB-12 data cannot be assumed for every B. lactis label.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.interpretation: B. bifidum commonly appears in multistrain formulas; per-strain CFU stays separate from total CFU.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.population: infants and children in labelled products
dose_contexts.interpretation: Age group and strain code need to remain visible because infant, child, and adult probiotic evidence differs.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.interpretation: R0175 is commonly studied with L. helveticus R0052; combined-product evidence should stay attached to both strain codes.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.population: children or adults depending on strain
dose_contexts.interpretation: Infant, child, and adult IBS contexts are different; the strain code determines whether a label is clinically comparable.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.population: adults and children in cold studies
dose_contexts.interpretation: The 8700:2 strain is commonly studied with L. plantarum HEAL9 at 1 billion CFU total per day; other paracasei strains need separate support.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.population: adults with IBS in trial settings
dose_contexts.interpretation: The 299v strain has IBS trial context around this daily dose; other L. plantarum strains need their own evidence.

dose_contexts.context: HEAL9 plus L. paracasei 8700:2 immune trial context
dose_contexts.population: adults and children in cold studies
dose_contexts.interpretation: HEAL9 is commonly studied as a paired strain with L. paracasei 8700:2, often at 1 billion CFU total per day.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.population: adults and children in labelled products
dose_contexts.interpretation: This species appears often in multistrain products; per-strain CFU and strain code are needed before mapping to a clinical trial context.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.population: children and adults in labelled products
dose_contexts.interpretation: L. casei appears in broad-spectrum formulas; per-strain CFU should not be replaced by the product total.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.

dose_contexts.population: children and adults in labelled products
dose_contexts.interpretation: Australian multistrain products list L. gasseri at modest per-strain CFU; strain code and formula context remain essential.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.context: R0052 plus B. longum R0175 context
dose_contexts.interpretation: R0052 is commonly studied with B. longum R0175; the strongest comparison keeps both strains together when interpreting gut-brain claims.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.population: children and adults in AAD studies
dose_contexts.interpretation: LGG evidence is strain-specific; labels that only say L. rhamnosus should stay less certain.

dose_contexts.population: adults and children in labelled products
dose_contexts.interpretation: HN001 appears in Australian practitioner labels at lower per-strain CFU inside multistrain formulas; this is discovery context, not a universal dose.

dose_contexts.population: infants and children in strain-labelled studies
dose_contexts.interpretation: L. reuteri interpretation depends on strain and age group; infant colic, oral health, and adult gut studies are separate evidence contexts.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.interpretation: Common Australian labels range from low single-billion products to 45-60 billion CFU multistrain capsules; strain identity still matters more than total CFU.
Very high CFU sachets appear in practitioner ranges; high potency prompts context review rather than an automatic stronger-benefit assumption.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.population: children and adults in AAD studies
dose_contexts.interpretation: S. boulardii is a probiotic yeast, so CFU and milligram labels may both appear; compare by the stated viable count when available.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.population: adults and children in labelled products
dose_contexts.interpretation: This species commonly appears as one strain within multistrain labels; the clinical context depends on the full formula and strain code.

clinical_notes.text: Genus, species, strain code, total CFU, per-strain CFU, and viability timing all matter. A higher CFU number does not prove a better fit.
Premature infants, critical illness, central venous catheters, immunocompromise, severe underlying disease, pregnancy, and infant use need clinical review.

dose_contexts.interpretation: There is no app NRV calculation for EPA/DHA. Preserve total omega-3, EPA, DHA, and ALA separately when labels provide them.

clinical_notes.text: Do not treat total fish oil as EPA plus DHA; labels often list oil mass and active fatty-acid mass separately.
High-dose EPA/DHA should be reviewed with anticoagulant or antiplatelet use, surgery plans, and clinician-directed therapy.

dose_contexts.interpretation: No NRV or universal supplement target applies; record mg dose and keep medication context separate from wellness claims.

clinical_notes.text: Acetylcysteine is also used as a medicine; do not infer a therapeutic indication from a supplement label.

dose_contexts.interpretation: No NRV or UL is bundled for taurine; keep the observed mg or g amount and avoid efficacy conclusions.

clinical_notes.text: Taurine is retained for OCR and label review, not for RDI or UL calculations.

dose_contexts.interpretation: No NRV applies; preserve the extract amount and any standardised flavonoid amount separately.

clinical_notes.text: If a label lists both citrus extract and hesperidin/rutin, keep the parent extract separate from the active-compound standardisation.

dose_contexts.interpretation: No NRV applies; record aglycone or compound form when a label distinguishes quercetin from quercetin dihydrate.

clinical_notes.text: Keep immune or inflammation claims as marketing context unless the UI later links them to indication-specific evidence.

dose_contexts.population: adults 19-50, female-to-male range where sex differs
dose_contexts.interpretation: Compare the effective daily Choline amount against the selected demographic in NRV data; this range is a quick adult context only.

dose_contexts.context: adult AU upper limit label-review threshold
dose_contexts.interpretation: Treat labels near or above this threshold as review flags; apply demographic-specific ULs in CalculationService.

clinical_notes.text: Use the observed label amount for Choline; if OCR misses the amount, keep it unknown rather than converting it to zero.
